title: Anisotropy in Near-Spherical Colloidal Nanoparticles
abstract: Two major aspects of functional colloidal nanoparticles are their colloidal stability (dispersion) and controlled assembly of nanoparticles into ordered structures. Simplifying colloidal nanoparticles as isotropically interacting spheres is unsuitable for small nanoparticles capped with hydrocarbon chain ligands in which the ligand−ligand interaction plays a prominent role in the assembly processes. However, experimentally characterizing the ligand shell structure in solution presents significant challenges, and computer simulations yield divergent results without effective validation. Moreover, the connection between detailed information regarding ligand shell structures and interparticle interactions, in relation to the diverse dynamical behaviors of colloidal nanoparticles, remains poorly understood. In this study, we reveal the relationship between the ligand shell structures, interparticle interactions, and dynamical behaviors of few-nm-sized near-spherical nanoparticles capped with hydrocarbon chain ligands immersed in nonpolar solvents. Our study shows a transformation of the interparticle interactions from anisotropic attractions to isotropic repulsions as a result of the change in the ligand shell structures from order to disorder caused by varying temperature and other factors. The interplay between anisotropic attractions from ligand bundles and isotropic repulsions from disordered ligands dictates the nanoparticle dynamical behaviors of dispersion, uncontrolled aggregation, and controlled assembly.
results: **Ligand Shell Structures.** We used FG simulations to predict the ligand shell structures of an isolated NP. We focus on near-spherical NPs with a core diameter smaller than 7 nm, whose core-core interaction is negligible.2 We began with a typical colloidal NP system of Au NPs capped with alkanethiols ( $C_nH_{2n+1}SH$ , n = 9-21, denoted  $SC_n$  hereafter) dispersed in hexane  $(C_6)$ , as shown in Figure 1a. A key point in the simulations of colloidal NPs is the choice of force field to describe the ligands and solvents. Based on the simulation and experimental results of the melting points of alkanes, ligand shell structures in a vacuum, and order-disorder transition temperatures of ligand shells, nine force fields were carefully evaluated (see section 5 in Supporting Information), and then we chose the Transferable Potentials for Phase Equilibria (TraPPE)<sup>30</sup> to describe the ligand-ligand, ligand-solvent, and solvent-solvent interactions (Table S1). Our FG simulations show that 441 octadecanethiol (SC<sub>18</sub>) molecules absorbed on the surface of an Au NP with a truncated octahedral shape and a diameter of 5.1 nm (grafting density: 5.5 nm<sup>2</sup>) crystallize into three to four ligand bundles at 300 K under vacuum<sup>19</sup> (Figure 1b). When the same capped NP is immersed in  $C_6$ , the capping ligands crystallize into eight bundles on the eight (111) surfaces of the truncated octahedron (TO) (Figure 1c). If the ligands are 9-octadecene-1-thiol (C<sub>18</sub>H<sub>35</sub>SH), they form a uniform shell with a completely disordered structure on the same NP in  $C_6$  (Figure 1d). The only difference between  $SC_{18}$ and C<sub>18</sub>H<sub>35</sub>SH is that the latter has a C=C double bond that bends the hydrocarbon chain. Crystallinities ( $	heta$ ), defined as the fraction of the ligand in the crystalline state (section 6 of Supporting Information), are 0.63 and 0.60 for the capping  $SC_{18}$  in a vacuum and  $C_6$  solvent, respectively, and 0.0 for the capping  $C_{18}H_{35}SH$  in  $C_6$ .
discussion: The previous hypothesis suggested that the hydrocarbon chain ligands interdigitated in a solution or vacuum. <sup>29,40</sup> However, our simulations demonstrate that ligand interdigitation is unstable (Figure S12), which aligns with other simulation studies. <sup>41</sup> We attribute the instability of the ligand interdigitation to the unfavorable entropy loss associated with this ligand shell structure. Intuitively, it is expected that the alkane tails of the ligands would be disordered in a nonpolar solvent due to the principle of "like dissolves like". Nevertheless, long alkane chain ligands can crystallize in a good solvent at room temperature, likely facilitated by the ordered packing of the head groups on the NP surfaces.
conclusion: This work reveals the relationship among the ligand shell structures, interparticle interactions, and dynamical behaviors of small-sized colloidal NPs in a nonpolar solvent. Near-spherical NPs capped by alkanethiol ligands may have a nonspherical shape in a good solvent through ligand crystallization, which causes strongly anisotropic interparticle interactions. The tight relationship between ligand shell structures and interparticle interactions distinguishes colloidal NPs from the ionic and molecular solutions (e.g., NaCl aqueous solutions). In these common solutions, the interionic or intermolecular interaction is determined by the electronic structures of the ions and molecules, and its strength and isotropy are typically temperature-independent. However, the ligand shell structures respond sensitively to the temperature and other factors, leading to dramatic changes in interparticle interactions. This property provides opportunities for delicate control of the motion and dynamic behavior of colloidal NPs.
